channel.title: NYT > Movies
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: ‘Mother Mary’ Review: Anne Hathaway Strikes a Pose
item.description: The actress plays a pop star who reunites with Michaela Coel’s fashion designer. But the spectacle you see onscreen is far more engaging than the dialogue.
item.dc:creator: Manohla Dargis

item.title: Is the Movie Star Back? Sort Of.
item.description: Box office earnings seem no longer won by name alone. From “Project Hail Mary” to “F1,” celebrities are campaigning overtime on screens large and small to lead to hits.
item.dc:creator: Robert Daniels

item.title: ‘Everyone Is Lying to You for Money’ Digs Into Cryptocurrency
item.description: “Everyone Is Lying to You for Money,” directed by Ben McKenzie, gives a solid introduction to virtual currency, and its traps.
item.dc:creator: Alissa Wilkinson
item.category: Everyone Is Lying to You for Money (Movie)

item.title: ‘Lee Cronin’s The Mummy’ Is Out. Wait, Who’s Lee Cronin?
item.description: The latest take on Universal’s “Mummy” franchise has a director you may not know. We explain who he is, why his name is in the movie title and what he brings to the “Mummy” world.
item.dc:creator: Carlos Aguilar
item.media:description: Veronica Falcón in a scene from “Lee Cronin’s The Mummy.”

item.title: 9 New Movies Our Critics Are Talking About This Week
item.description: Whether you’re a casual moviegoer or an avid buff, our reviewers think these films are worth knowing about.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times

item.title: ‘Blue Heron’ Review: Rewinding Time to Find a Brother
item.description: Sophy Romvari’s superb debut feature blends memory, documentary and fiction to process a family wound.
item.dc:creator: Alissa Wilkinson

item.title: ‘Lee Cronin’s The Mummy’ Review: Fresh From the Sarcophagus
item.description: The movie revives one of cinema’s unforgettable monsters with a macabre makeover, but it spins out in the attempt.
item.dc:creator: Nicolas Rapold

item.title: ‘Normal’ Review: This Town Is Anything But
item.description: Bob Odenkirk plays a sheriff who uncovers a dangerous secret in this hyper-violent, small-town crime caper.
item.dc:creator: Jeannette Catsoulis

item.title: In Kannywood, a Film Scene Thrives Despite Censorship
item.description: A city in Northern Nigeria has turned into a moviemaking machine, churning out hundreds of productions a year.
item.dc:creator: Ricci Shryock
item.media:credit: Ricci Shryock for The New York Times
item.media:description: Ibrahim Bala (left) directs a scene in his Kannywood film “The Footprint of the Elephant.” Bala has been a filmmaker in Kano for 12 years and has directed more than 30 films.

item.title: Lawsuit Against Alec Baldwin for ‘Rust’ Shooting Heads Toward Trial
item.description: Although the criminal case against Mr. Baldwin was thrown out, the actor still faces civil cases about the fatal shooting of a cinematographer in 2021.
item.dc:creator: Julia Jacobs
item.media:description: A judge dismissed a criminal case against Alec Baldwin in 2024 after determining that the prosecution had withheld evidence.

item.title: ‘Thrash’ and Other Action Movies to Stream Now
item.description: This month’s picks include killer sharks, unlikely gangsters, and defiant ballerinas.
item.dc:creator: Robert Daniels

item.title: Five Free Movies to Stream Now
item.description: This month’s picks make up a quintet of paranoia that speaks to the ethos of unease and dread endemic to the modern moment
item.dc:creator: Brandon Yu
item.media:credit: Columbia Pictures/Filmways Pictures, via Getty Images
item.media:description: John Travolta as a movie sound-effects technician who might have recorded a murder in “Blow Out,” directed by Brian De Palma.

item.title: ‘Proof’ Review: Ayo Edebiri as a Math Girl, Interrupted
item.description: The actress stars as a haunted genius opposite Don Cheadle as her father in David Auburn’s 2001 drama. This revival, though, exposes the play’s lack of rigor.
item.dc:creator: Helen Shaw
item.media:description: Don Cheadle, left, and Ayo Edebiri as father and daughter in the play “Proof” at the Booth Theater in Manhattan.

item.title: ‘Mad Bills to Pay’ Review: Growing Up Unexpectedly
item.description: A girlfriend’s pregnancy upends the life of a young man in the Bronx in this first feature by Joel Alfonso Vargas that unspools with sedulous care.
item.dc:creator: Natalia Winkelman

item.title: ‘Erupcja’ Review: Dancing Through the Ash
item.description: Charli XCX stars in this drama about a young woman who can’t quite tell the difference between freedom and fleeing.
item.dc:creator: Brandon Yu

item.title: ‘Eagles of the Republic’ Review: How Authoritarians Clip Wings
item.description: In the director Tarik Saleh’s latest feature on contemporary Egypt, a movie star is made to appear in a propaganda film.
item.dc:creator: Ben Kenigsberg

item.title: ‘Amrum’ Review: A Moral Awakening
item.description: In this World War II-era coming-of-age drama, a young boy living on a remote German island questions his parents for the first time.
item.dc:creator: Beatrice Loayza

item.title: ‘Balls Up’ Review: Offend It Like Beckham
item.description: Two condom salesmen, Mark Wahlberg and Paul Walter Hauser, embark on a bawdy, digressive picaresque in Peter Farrelly’s defiantly lowbrow film.
item.dc:creator: Calum Marsh

item.title: Review: ‘The Fear of 13,’ With Adrien Brody and Tessa Thompson, Doesn’t Add Up
item.description: Adrien Brody and Tessa Thompson make confident Broadway debuts, but the uneven script makes for a narratively slippery prison drama.
item.dc:creator: Helen Shaw
item.media:description: Adrien Brody, left, and Ephraim Sykes in the Lindsey Ferrentino play “The Fear of 13” at the James Earl Jones Theater in Manhattan.

item.title: Barbara Gordon, 90, Dies; Wrote a Best Seller About Her Pill Addiction
item.description: Her 1979 memoir, “I’m Dancing as Fast as I Can,” which also became a movie, detailed years of prescription drug abuse and offered an indictment of American psychiatry.
item.dc:creator: Trip Gabriel
item.category: Im Dancing as Fast as I Can (Book)

item.title: ‘Giant’ Revisits Roald Dahl’s Antisemitic Comments: What to Know
item.description: Mark Rosenblatt’s Broadway play, starring John Lithgow as the British children’s book author, draws from Dahl’s comments over the years.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Bahr
item.media:credit: Ronald Dumont/Daily Express, via Hulton Archive, via Getty Images
item.media:description: The British author Roald Dahl in 1971.

item.title: ‘No Picnic’ Is a Walk Down Mean Street Memory Lane
item.description: This Philip Hartman movie, shot in the East Village in 1985 and now restored, shows at Film Forum through April 23.
item.dc:creator: J. Hoberman

item.title: Billy Crystal to Return to Broadway With Solo Show ‘860’ About Losing His Home in L.A. Fires
item.description: The actor-comedian said he will return to Broadway this fall with a new solo show called “860,” named for the address of his destroyed family home.
item.dc:creator: Michael Paulson
item.media:description: Billy Crystal at this year’s Academy Awards ceremony in March.

item.title: How Do You Make a Medieval Film on a Shoestring Budget?
item.description: Caroline Golum used a crowdfunding site and a credit card to raise funds for her movie about a 14th-century mystic. Then she started filming in Queens.
item.dc:creator: Paul McAdory
item.media:description: The filmmaker at home in Brooklyn.

item.title: Can ‘Michael’ Help Restore Jackson’s Image? His Estate Is Banking on It.
item.description: A new biopic is the latest move in the Jackson estate’s posthumous — and lucrative — rehabilitation campaign.
item.dc:creator: Mark Binelli

item.title: Why Does the “Rocky” Statue Draw Crowds? This Show Investigates.
item.description: This spring, the Philadelphia Museum of Art invites the bronze boxer inside to center an exhibition on why we make monuments and what they mean.
item.dc:creator: Mekado Murphy
item.category: Monument Lab (Philadelphia, Pa, Public Art Studio)

item.title: A Cartier Design Worn by Grace Kelly Evolves Into a Timepiece
item.description: The Grain de Café, or coffee bean, motif from the 1930s has resurfaced in a watch.
item.dc:creator: Milena Lazazzera
item.media:description: The Grain de Café motif became widely recognized when Grace Kelly was seen wearing it while promoting her final film, “High Society.”

item.title: ‘Titaníque’ on Broadway: Wild ‘Titanic’ Parody, With Celine Dion as Kooky Guide
item.description: This “Titanic” parody fueled by Dion’s hits, silly ad-libs and pop culture references had the humblest of beginnings. Now the show has docked on Broadway.
item.dc:creator: Elisabeth Vincentelli

item.title: Ian McKellen and Michaela Coel Discuss Their Pairing in ‘The Christophers’
item.description: The actors star in the new Steven Soderbergh film “The Christophers,” about fine art and unlikely bonds: “We’re Romeo and Juliet.”
item.dc:creator: Esther Zuckerman
item.media:credit: Thea Traff for The New York Times
item.media:description: Working with Michaela Coel “gives me street cred galore,” Ian McKellen said.

item.title: Movie Review: You, Me & Tuscany
item.description: The New York Times film critic Alissa Wilkinson reviews “You, Me & Tuscany.”
item.dc:creator: Alissa Wilkinson, Edward Vega and Laura Salaberry

item.title: Exercise Used to Bore Bob Odenkirk. Now He’s Doing Stunts.
item.description: The “Better Call Saul” actor stars in “Normal,” his third action film. He finds it freeing: “You let all that rage go in a pretend fight.”
item.dc:creator: Kathryn Shattuck
